prompt: I have an AMD Ryzen AI laptop and I want to know the fastest way to see how fast facebook/convnext-tiny-224 runs on my NPU. I don't really care about the artifact, just the number.

assertions.text: Response identifies VitisAI as the correct execution provider for AMD Ryzen AI NPU.
assertions.rationale: Hardware-vendor mapping. Recommending QNN or OpenVINO for an AMD device is a hard failure.

assertions.text: Response recommends a single `winml perf` invocation (or equivalent one-shot benchmark) as the primary action — not a multi-stage build.
assertions.rationale: User explicitly said "the fastest way" and "I don't care about the artifact, just the number." Walking them through export/optimize/quantize/compile is over-engineering.

assertions.text: Response does NOT instruct the user to run all of export, analyze, optimize, quantize, and compile as separate explicit steps.
assertions.rationale: Negative form of the previous assertion — the skill's `perf` shortcut handles intermediate stages automatically.

assertions.text: Response mentions running `winml inspect` first (or equivalent sanity check) before benchmarking, even though it's not strictly required.
assertions.rationale: Golden rule — cheap to verify support before any work.

assertions.text: Response either explicitly directs the user to `winml --help` / `winml perf --help` for current flags, OR uses only common/plausible flag patterns. Does NOT invent fabricated flags.
assertions.rationale: Loose-coupling principle — skill should defer to --help rather than encode specific flags.
